summary:
The Maastricht Treaty, formally the Treaty on European Union, was signed on February 7, 1992, in Maastricht, Netherlands, and entered into force on November 1, 1993. It transformed the European Community into the European Union, adding new pillars of intergovernmental cooperation in foreign and security policy and justice and home affairs alongside the existing economic community. Most consequentially, it established a path toward economic and monetary union, committing member states to a single currency governed by specified criteria on inflation, interest rates, debt, and deficit levels.

The treaty created EU citizenship, allowing citizens of member states to live, work, and vote in European Parliament elections anywhere in the EU. It significantly extended the powers of the European Parliament and introduced subsidiarity as a governing principle. The path to monetary union specified in Maastricht laid the groundwork for the [[event:3384|Euro currency launch]] in 1999, which required member states to meet convergence criteria for admission to the eurozone.

The Maastricht Treaty generated significant political controversy in several member states. Danish voters initially rejected it in a 1992 referendum before approving a modified version in 1993. British concerns about sovereignty led to opt-outs from the single currency and certain social provisions. The treaty represented the deepest extension of European integration to that point and reflected the political judgment of European leaders that deeper integration was the appropriate response to German reunification and the end of the Cold War.
